content.text: Executive Steering Committee Structure

The Executive Steering Committee (ESC) is the governance body that gives a Claude program organizational authority, budget accountability, and strategic direction. Without a functioning ESC, the CoE operates as a technology project rather than a business program — it can build things, but it cannot resolve cross-BU conflicts, redirect budget, or make the organizational changes that scaling Claude requires.
This document covers how to structure an ESC, who should sit on it, what decisions it owns, and how to run it effectively across the lifecycle of a Claude program.
Why the ESC Is Not Optional

Many enterprise AI programs start without a formal steering committee — the CoE reports to a single executive sponsor and operates informally. This works in Phase 1 (1–2 BUs, small team, limited budget). It fails in Phase 2 and beyond for predictable reasons:
Cross-BU conflicts have no resolution mechanism:

when two BUs compete for CoE engineering time, or one BU's use case creates risk for another, the CoE cannot resolve this unilaterally. Without a governance body with authority over all affected BUs, these conflicts either stall or get resolved based on who has the most informal influence — neither is systematic.
Budget decisions outgrow the sponsor:

a single executive sponsor can approve Phase 1 investment from their own budget. Phase 2 and 3 budgets typically require CFO involvement, cross-BU cost allocation, or steering committee approval. Without the ESC as the budget authority, scaling requires repeated ad hoc approvals.
Risk decisions need multiple authorities:

a high-stakes Tier 3 use case involving customer data, legal review, and production deployment touches Legal, CISO, a BU leader, and Finance. No single executive sponsor can approve on behalf of all of these. The ESC brings these authorities together in one governance body.
Program visibility creates accountability:

when the CEO asks how the Claude program is progressing, there needs to be an authoritative answer. The ESC is the body that owns that answer — it aggregates reporting from the CoE and presents a unified program view to the board and C-suite.
The ESC is not a rubber stamp. It is the body that resolves the decisions the CoE cannot make on its own — budget, cross-BU priority, organizational change, and risk acceptance.
ESC Composition
Required Members
The ESC should include one representative from each of these organizational functions:
Role
ESC Title
Why Required
Executive Sponsor
ESC Chair
Provides organizational authority and budget ownership for the program. Without a C-suite chair, the ESC lacks the authority to make binding decisions.
Business Unit Heads (1–2 representatives)
BU Member
Bring BU priorities, surface use case demand, commit BU resources for adoption. Representation should rotate or expand as more BUs join the program.
CFO or Finance VP
Finance Member
Approves budget, reviews ROI cases, signs off on investment theses. Without Finance in the room, ESC decisions often require a separate Finance approval cycle.
General Counsel or CLO
Legal Member
Approves use of Claude for legally sensitive content, reviews contract templates, manages IP and liability exposure.
CISO or VP Security
Security Member
Approves data handling practices, security architecture, and incident response plans. Required for any Tier 3 use case.
CHRO or VP HR
HR Member
Required for any use case affecting employee data, performance management, or workforce implications of AI-driven productivity.
CoE Director
Secretary / Presenter
Prepares and presents the agenda, reports on program status, and executes ESC decisions. Does not vote on decisions affecting the CoE directly.
Optional Members
Depending on the organization's structure and the Claude program's scope, these roles may be appropriate:
Chief Data Officer (CDO):
if the organization has a CDO, they should be on the ESC — data governance is central to Claude deployment decisions and the CDO often has authority the CISO and Legal teams defer to.
Chief Risk Officer (CRO):
for financial services, healthcare, or other regulated industries where AI risk is a regulatory concern, the CRO brings a risk management lens that complements Legal and Security.
External AI Advisor:

some organizations include an external AI expert (academic, consultant, or industry peer) as a non-voting observer. This provides independent perspective and is particularly valuable in early phases when the organization is still building internal AI expertise.
Employee Representative:
for organizations with strong employee relations commitments, including a works council or employee representative as an observer builds trust that AI deployment decisions consider worker interests.
Size Constraints
An ESC with more than 10 members struggles to make decisions efficiently. If the natural membership exceeds 10:
Rotate BU representation: instead of all BU heads attending every meeting, rotate to include the 2–3 BUs most relevant to the current quarter's agenda
Use a tiered structure: a core ESC of 5–7 members makes decisions; an extended ESC of additional stakeholders receives updates quarterly but does not attend every meeting
Split Legal and Security into working group roles: the CISO and CLO join the ESC for decisions that require their authority, but attend working groups (not every ESC meeting) for operational matters
ESC Decision Rights: What the ESC Owns

The most common ESC design failure is unclear decision rights — the ESC exists but no one knows which decisions require ESC approval versus CoE approval versus sponsor approval. Define decision rights explicitly:
Decision Type
ESC Vote Required?
Rationale
Annual program budget approval
Yes — full ESC
Cross-functional budget commitment
Phase gate approval (Phase 1→2, 2→3)
Yes — full ESC
Signals program-wide commitment and resource reallocation
Tier 3 use case approval (new)
Yes — Legal + Security + BU Member minimum
High-stakes deployment requires multi-authority sign-off
Mandatory kill of a use case (Category 1)
No — CoE Director authority
Safety/compliance kills must be immediate; ESC notified within 24h
Performance-based sunset (Category 2/3)
No — CoE Director with BU owner concurrence
Operational decision; ESC informed at next quarterly meeting
New BU onboarding to Claude program
Yes — ESC Chair + Finance
New BU means new resource commitment
Vendor contract > $500K
Yes — Finance + ESC Chair
Exceeds CoE procurement authority
Organizational change (new CoE role, restructure)
Yes — full ESC + CHRO
Workforce implications require HR and full ESC
Risk policy exception
Yes — Legal + Security + ESC Chair
Exceptions to policy require the authorities that set the policy
Use case tier reclassification (up)
Yes — CoE Director + relevant ESC members
Upward reclassification increases governance burden on BU
Use case tier reclassification (down)
No — CoE Director authority
Downward reclassification reduces burden; notify ESC quarterly

The CoE Director should maintain a 'decision log' — a running record of every decision made under each authority level. This prevents decision authority from drifting over time and provides an audit trail for governance reviews.
ESC Meeting Cadence and Structure
Quarterly Full ESC Meeting (90 minutes)
The core ESC meeting runs quarterly and covers the full program:

# Quarterly ESC Agenda Template (90 min)

0:00 — Program Status (15 min)
  Presenter: CoE Director
  Content: Portfolio summary (use cases by tier and status), 
           adoption metrics, ROI summary, budget vs. actuals
  Output: Executive awareness; any urgent escalations raised

0:15 — Phase Progress and Next Phase Gate (20 min)
  Presenter: CoE Director + relevant BU lead
  Content: Progress against current phase milestones,
           blockers, phase gate criteria status
  Output: Decision to proceed, hold, or replan

0:35 — Investment Review (15 min)
  Presenter: CoE Director + Finance Member
  Content: Quarterly spend, ROI cases closed,
           budget request for next quarter
  Output: Budget approval or modification

0:50 — Tier 3 Use Case Approvals (20 min)
  Presenter: CoE Director + BU champion per use case
  Content: Use case brief, risk assessment, 
           data handling plan, rollout approach
  Output: Approval, conditional approval, or deferral per use case

1:10 — Strategic Direction and Emerging Issues (15 min)
  Presenter: ESC Chair
  Content: External AI developments affecting strategy,
           organizational AI policy updates,
           competitive context if material
  Output: Any strategic direction changes for CoE to incorporate

1:25 — AOB and Next Meeting Date (5 min)
Monthly Working Group Meetings
Between quarterly ESC meetings, three working groups handle operational governance:
Technical Working Group (bi-weekly, 60 min):

CoE engineers + Security + relevant IT. Covers architecture decisions, integration issues, security review of in-flight use cases, API and infrastructure management. Does not make policy decisions — escalates policy questions to ESC.
Use Case Working Group (monthly, 60 min):
CoE PMs + BU champions. Reviews the use case pipeline (intake, prioritization, status), surfaces adoption blockers, tracks time-to-production for each active use case.
Governance Working Group (monthly, 60 min):
CoE Director + Legal + HR + Risk. Reviews policy questions, risk exceptions, policy updates needed for new use case types, and incident response readiness.
Ad Hoc ESC Convening
Define the conditions under which the ESC can be convened outside the quarterly schedule:
Category 1 (mandatory kill) trigger: CoE Director convenes relevant ESC members (Legal + Security minimum) within 24 hours
Budget request > 20% above approved quarterly budget: Finance Member + ESC Chair within 5 business days
Material AI incident (accuracy failure affecting > 100 users, or data breach): full ESC within 48 hours
Regulatory inquiry related to AI use: Legal Member + ESC Chair immediately

Ad hoc convening should be rare — if it happens more than once per quarter, it is a signal that the quarterly agenda is not sufficient and should be expanded, or that the program is generating more governance events than its maturity level can absorb.
Onboarding the ESC: Getting the First Meeting Right

The first ESC meeting sets the tone for the entire program's governance. Most CoE directors make one of two mistakes: they over-brief (overwhelming executives with technical detail) or they under-brief (treating the first meeting as a rubber stamp). A well-structured first meeting:
Send pre-read materials 5 business days before:

a 2-page program brief covering: what is Claude, why this program exists, what the first phase will cost and deliver, and what the ESC's role is. Executives who read the pre-read arrive able to engage substantively; those who do not still have the one-pager to anchor the discussion.
Open with the 'why now' argument (10 minutes):

the ESC Chair should briefly explain why AI capability-building is a strategic priority and why doing it through a structured CoE program is better than ad hoc BU experimentation. This is not a Claude sales pitch — it is the strategic context that makes the governance commitment make sense.
Explain the decision rights model (15 minutes):

review the decision rights table. Ask each ESC member to confirm they understand what decisions they will be asked to make and that they can make those decisions within their authority. Clarify any scope questions before the program is in flight.
Approve Phase 1 (15 minutes):

the first ESC meeting should close with a concrete decision: approved to proceed with Phase 1 with a defined budget, timeline, and success criteria. A first meeting that ends without a decision is a missed opportunity to establish governance momentum.
Confirm communication norms (5 minutes):

how does the CoE Director escalate to the ESC Chair between meetings? What response time is expected for email escalations? Who is the designated backup if an ESC member is unavailable for a time-sensitive decision?
ESC Chair Selection: The Most Important Governance Decision

The effectiveness of the ESC depends more on the Chair than on any other structural factor. A Chair who attends but does not engage produces a ceremonial committee. A Chair who engages produces a governing body.
Characteristics of an effective ESC Chair:
Organizational authority to commit cross-functional resources:
the Chair must be able to direct BU heads, Finance, Legal, and Security to engage with the program. A CTO who can only speak for IT is insufficient; a COO who can direct all operational BUs is ideal.
Genuine belief in the program's value:

a Chair who privately doubts the program will not defend it when it encounters difficulty. Identify an executive who has personally experienced AI productivity gains or who has a strategic commitment to AI capability-building.
Willingness to make difficult calls:

the ESC Chair will eventually need to kill a BU leader's favorite use case, redirect budget from a BU that is underperforming, or override a department head's resistance to governance. A Chair who avoids conflict cannot execute these decisions.
Time commitment:

the quarterly ESC meeting requires 90 minutes of preparation time plus the meeting itself. The Chair should also be reachable for ad hoc escalations with a 24-hour response expectation. Confirm this commitment before appointing the Chair.

The ESC Chair is the program's organizational sponsor. The difference between a program that scales and one that stalls is usually not the CoE team — it is whether the Chair will make the hard calls when the program encounters organizational resistance.
Reporting from CoE to ESC

The CoE Director is responsible for preparing a quarterly program report for the ESC. The report should be written to support the ESC's decision-making role, not to demonstrate CoE effort. A good quarterly report:
Leads with the metric that matters most to the ESC: total annualized value delivered by active use cases
Shows a traffic-light status for each active use case — green (on track), yellow (monitoring), red (in remediation or at risk)
Presents the phase gate status honestly — if the phase gate criteria will not be met, say so and propose options
Includes a risk section: what are the top 3 risks to the program in the next quarter, and what is the CoE doing about each
Closes with a clear 'decisions needed' section — a bulleted list of the specific decisions the ESC needs to make at this meeting

Avoid reporting vanity metrics to the ESC: number of prompts run, number of API calls, number of workshops held. These demonstrate activity, not value. ESC members are executives — they respond to business outcomes: cost saved, revenue influenced, time freed, risk reduced.
ESC Maturity: Evolving the Committee as the Program Scales

The ESC that serves a Phase 1 program is not the ESC that should govern a Phase 3 federated program. As the program scales, the committee must evolve in membership, meeting structure, and decision rights.
Phase 1 ESC: Lightweight Oversight (Months 1–6)

In Phase 1, the ESC functions as a program review board rather than a full governance body. The program is small, decisions are straightforward, and the goal is to establish governance habits, not to build an elaborate committee structure.
3–5 members: Executive Sponsor (Chair), CoE Director, one BU head, Finance, and Legal minimum
Monthly meetings of 45 minutes — quarterly is too infrequent when the program is new and decisions arise weekly
Agenda focused on: milestone status, first use case approval, and budget tracking
No formal working groups yet — working group functions handled informally by the CoE team

The Phase 1 ESC is also building trust. ESC members need to understand what Claude can and cannot do, what the CoE team is capable of, and what governance feels like in practice. Monthly meetings in Phase 1 accelerate this learning.
Phase 2 ESC: Operational Governance (Months 7–18)
By Phase 2, the program has multiple use cases in production, at least 2–3 BUs engaged, and real budget flowing. The ESC expands and its structure becomes more formal:
Expand to 7–9 members: add CISO, CHRO, and a second BU head representative
Shift to quarterly ESC meetings (program is stable enough), supplemented by monthly working groups
Establish formal working groups: Technical, Use Case, and Governance
Introduce a formal program report template — the CoE Director presents the same format every quarter for easy trend comparison
Formalize the decision log: every ESC decision is recorded with the date, attendees, options considered, and resolution
Phase 3 ESC: Strategic Governance (Months 19+)

In Phase 3, the program is federated — BUs have embedded AI engineers, use cases are being built and run outside the CoE, and the ESC's role shifts from operational oversight to strategic direction and policy governance.
ESC membership evolves: BU heads may be replaced by BU AI leads who have direct program accountability
The CoE Director's role on the ESC evolves from presenter to secretary — the CoE is now executing rather than building, and strategy is increasingly set by the ESC rather than proposed by the CoE
Consider adding an external AI board observer: an independent expert who provides competitive benchmarking and challenge to internal assumptions
Introduce an annual ESC offsite (half-day) to review the program's 3-year strategic plan — quarterly meetings are too compressed for strategic planning discussions
The ESC begins reporting to the board of directors on AI program status, typically through the Audit or Risk committee

The ESC that treats Phase 3 like Phase 1 will create governance overhead without value. Mature programs need strategic governance, not operational governance. The committee's job is to set the direction, not to review every use case.
Handling Common ESC Dysfunctions
Even well-designed ESCs develop dysfunction over time. Recognizing the pattern and applying the appropriate correction is part of the CoE Director's governance responsibility.
The Rubber Stamp Committee
The committee meets, the CoE presents, everyone nods, and no substantive decisions are made. This happens when:
Decisions that should require ESC approval are being made by the CoE unilaterally (scope creep of CoE authority)
Pre-meeting alignment has been so thorough that there is nothing left to decide in the meeting
ESC members do not feel empowered to challenge CoE recommendations

Correction: introduce a mandatory 'challenge' agenda item — 15 minutes where ESC members are explicitly asked what they would do differently, what concerns they have not yet raised, and what they see the CoE missing. Frame it as a quality control mechanism, not a criticism of the CoE.
The Absent Chair

The ESC Chair delegates to a deputy or sends apologies frequently. Without the Chair, the committee cannot make binding decisions — attendees either defer items or make decisions that later get revisited when the Chair is present.

Correction: the ESC Chair must personally confirm their attendance for every quarterly meeting at least 3 weeks in advance. If the Chair cannot attend, the meeting is rescheduled (within 2 weeks) rather than delegated. Create this as an explicit norm in the ESC charter.
The Agenda Creep Committee

The ESC agenda expands over time to include operational status updates, vendor demos, and working group reports that belong elsewhere. The 90-minute meeting fills with information transfer and has no time for decisions.
Institute an 'information-only' rule: any agenda item that requires no decision is cut. Operational status goes in the pre-read; ESC time is for decisions.
Assign each agenda item a decision output: if an item cannot be specified as 'decide X' or 'approve Y,' it does not belong on the ESC agenda
Reserve the last 10 minutes of every meeting for 'decisions made this meeting' — reading back the decisions confirms they were actually made and creates an immediate record
The Siloed Committee
ESC members show up to protect their function's interests (Legal says no to everything; Finance asks only about cost) rather than to govern the program as a shared organizational asset.

Correction: this is a culture problem, not a structure problem — but structure can help. Introduce shared accountability metrics: ESC members are measured not just on their function's performance but on a shared program outcome (total annualized value delivered). When Legal, Finance, and Security all have a stake in the program's success, their instinct shifts from blocking to enabling.
ESC Charter: The Governing Document

Every ESC should operate under a written charter — a short document (2–4 pages) that defines the committee's purpose, membership, decision rights, meeting cadence, and amendment process. The charter serves several purposes:
Creates clarity when new members join: they can read the charter and understand the committee's role without relying on institutional memory
Resolves disputes about authority: when the CoE and a BU disagree about who makes a decision, the charter is the reference point
Signals organizational seriousness: a program with a written governance charter is taken more seriously by auditors, regulators, and the board than one operating on informal agreements

# Claude ESC Charter — Outline Template

1. PURPOSE
   The Claude Executive Steering Committee (ESC) provides strategic 
   governance, budget oversight, and organizational authority for the 
   Claude AI program. The ESC ensures the program delivers business 
   value in alignment with company strategy and within acceptable 
   risk parameters.

2. MEMBERSHIP
   Chair: [Executive Sponsor title]
   Members: [list roles]
   Secretary: CoE Director (non-voting on matters affecting the CoE)
   Term: Indefinite; Chair appointment reviewed annually

3. DECISION AUTHORITY
   [Insert decision rights table]
   Decisions not listed require ESC Chair judgment on required approval level.

4. MEETING CADENCE
   Full ESC: Quarterly, 90 minutes
   Working Groups: Monthly (Technical, Use Case, Governance)
   Ad Hoc: Per triggers defined in Section 3

5. QUORUM
   Decisions require: Chair + Finance + Legal (minimum)
   For Tier 3 approvals: Chair + Finance + Legal + Security + relevant BU head

6. REPORTING
   CoE Director submits quarterly program report 5 business days 
   before each ESC meeting.
   ESC Chair reports program status to Board Audit/Risk Committee annually.

7. AMENDMENTS
   Charter amendments require full ESC approval.
   Last amended: [date]
Integrating the ESC with Broader AI Governance

The Claude ESC does not operate in isolation. Most enterprises are simultaneously building governance for other AI systems — predictive models, RPA, data science tools, and now multiple generative AI platforms. The Claude ESC should be designed to connect with, rather than duplicate, this broader AI governance:
If an enterprise AI governance committee exists:

the Claude ESC should be structured as a sub-committee or working group of the broader AI governance body, not a parallel structure. Shared policies (data handling, AI ethics, incident response) should be defined at the enterprise level and applied consistently to Claude and other AI systems.
If no enterprise AI governance exists:

the Claude ESC may become the de facto enterprise AI governance body over time — the first to establish governance norms that other AI programs will follow. Design the ESC charter with this expansion in mind: use policy language that generalizes beyond Claude, and include decision rights that could extend to other AI platforms.
Regulatory alignment:

as AI regulation matures (EU AI Act, sector-specific guidance, state laws), the ESC is the body responsible for ensuring the Claude program remains compliant. The Legal member should track regulatory developments and bring material obligations to the ESC's attention at least one quarter before they take effect.
Summary

The Executive Steering Committee is the organizational backbone of an enterprise Claude program. Structure it with a C-suite Chair, cross-functional membership (Finance, Legal, Security, BU heads, HR), and clear decision rights that distinguish ESC authority from CoE authority. Run it quarterly with a structured 90-minute agenda, support it with monthly working groups for operational governance, and convene ad hoc for material incidents or budget escalations. The first ESC meeting sets the tone — close it with a Phase 1 approval and confirmed decision rights. Select the Chair based on organizational authority, genuine commitment, and willingness to make difficult calls. Report to the ESC in business outcomes, not activity metrics.
